records.ety: [OE. bever, AS. beofer, befer; akin to D. bever, OHG. bibar, G. biber, Sw. bäfver, Dan. bæver, Lith. bebru, Russ. bobr', Gael. beabhar, Corn. befer, L. fiber, and Skr. babhrus large ichneumon; also as an adj., brown, the animal being probably named from its color. √253. See Brown.]

defs: An amphibious rodent, of the genus Castor.
notes: ☞ It has palmated hind feet, and a broad, flat tail. It is remarkable for its ingenuity in constructing its lodges or “houses,” and dams across streams. It is valued for its fur, and for the material called castor, obtained from two small bags in the groin of the animal. The European species is Castor fiber, and the American is generally considered a variety of this, although sometimes called Castor Canadensis.

defs: A hat, formerly made of the fur of the beaver, but now usually of silk.
quotes: A brown beaver slouched over his eyes.

defs: Beaver cloth, a heavy felted woolen cloth, used chiefly for making overcoats.
The hair on a woman's pubic area; -- vulgar.
A person who works enthusiastically and diligently; -- used especially in the phrase eager beaver.

records.ety: [OE. baviere, bauier, beavoir, bever; fr. F. bavire, fr. bave slaver, drivel, foam, OF., prattle, drivel, perh. orig. an imitative word. Bavire, according to Cotgrave, is the bib put before a (slavering) child.]

defs: That piece of armor which protected the lower part of the face, whether forming a part of the helmet or fixed to the breastplate. It was so constructed (with joints or otherwise) that the wearer could raise or lower it to eat and drink.
